reasoning: The ECB is currently undergoing quantitative tightening (QT), letting APP and PEPP portfolios run off without reinvestment. This introduces a structural downward trend in ECBASSETSW, averaging over €10 billion per week. Recently, the balance sheet experienced an end-of-Q3 liquidity spike (jumping to 6,209,218 in early October) and has been steadily draining since (reaching 6,195,379 by Oct 24). Over the shortest horizon (Nov 2), the structural drain is highly likely to continue pulling the value below the Oct 26 benchmark, though short-term weekly volatility (e.g., MRO fluctuations) implies a small chance of a temporary bump. Over the medium horizon (Nov 25), the structural drag continues, but macro uncertainty begins to grow. For the furthest horizon (Jan 24, 2026), uncertainty is highest: while the baseline implies a lower balance sheet, tail risks—such as year-end/new-year liquidity operations or a shift in ECB policy (e.g., stopping QT or offering new targeted lending programs to counter weak economic growth and a 2.75% rate environment)—pull the probability much closer to 0.5. In accordance with the instruction to increase uncertainty across forecast horizons, I scale these probabilities progressively closer to 50%.
